entry.title: Re: SDI replication of a table from SAP ECC to Hana Cloud, using MssqlECCAdapter
entry.content:
I believe the issue is related to the pushdown processing on the source database. In the *.hdbrepltask projection, I'm using a CASE expression and the TO_DATE function, which do not appear to be supported by the source database.

When I remove these transformations and perform a straight 1:1 replication, the replication runs successfully.

The downside is that the required transformations must then be implemented in a separate flowgraph. However, this approach is acceptable and is probably the more appropriate architecture, as it keeps the replication task focused on data movement while handling transformations downstream.

entry.title: Confirmation Required: Released API/CDS View for Customer Hierarchy Change Documents
entry.content:
Hello SAP Support Team,We are currently designing an outbound integration from SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to an external system to send Customer Hierarchy data.As there is no standard API available for Customer Hierarchies, our current approach is to create a custom CDS view based on the standard CDS views:

  • I_CustUnivHierarchy
  • I_CustUnivHierarchyNode


This custom CDS view would then be exposed as a custom API to the external system.However, we have a delta requirement for this interface. The external system should receive only:

  • Newly created hierarchy records
  • Updated hierarchy records
  • Deleted hierarchy records


The challenge is that the CDS views I_CustUnivHierarchy and I_CustUnivHierarchyNode do not appear to provide change tracking information, such as creation date, last changed date, deletion indicator, or change type.In the Manage Customer Hierarchies Fiori app, we can see change history information

entry.title: Multilevel Custom As-Built Report in SAP PEO
entry.content:
[Blog Type: Hands-on Expertise]

 

Summary

This blog post describes an approach of how to implement in SAP S/4HANA a custom solution of the As-Built Report to cover some missing scenarios with traceable products on discrete industries customers.

The target audience for this blog post will be consulting people, project team members, key users, and subject matter experts who are interested on production engineering and operations within SAP S/4HANA.

 

Introduction

With the standard PEO app “As-Built Report” (transaction MPE_AS_BUILT), you can generate an as-built report for serialized products to get a top-down view of which components (planned and unplanned) have been consumed in an assembly and in any of the subassemblies. Based on an order number or serial number and material, you can check what has been built up to the current or a selected date.

The report is multilevel, which means that you can drill down into subassemblies of a parent assembly and view key data for each material. If you identify that the wrong serial number, batch number, or quantity was recorded for an assembled material, you can correct this data to resolve any inconsistencies.

With the standard as-built report, you can only drill down into subassemblies produced with shop floor orders (the so-called shop floor items), since the report does not list components assembled in classic production orders or components used for subcontracted assemblies (not PEO). The standard PEO As-Built Report is based on MPE tables and tree visualization is achieved by using an interactive hierarchical ALV, so the report is not based on Fiori.

On discrete industries customers there are often some scenarios with traceable products which are not managed with shop floor items, so they are not covered by the standard As-Built Report. Just to name a few of these possible scenarios:

  • Purchasing subcontracting.
  • Rework modifications.
  • Serial number changes.
  • Elements not in SAP S/4HANA (second-level components).

The purpose of this blog post is to describe the final adopted approach on one of the customers for implementing the As-Built custom solution to cover the missing scenarios. Customer was on SAP S/4HANA release 2021when this delivery was done.

 

Standard As-Built Report

The selection screen of the standard As-Built Report allows to select whether you want to generate the as-built report for a production order or material and serial number. Furthermore, you can also select whether you want to output all assemblies or assemblies built until a specific date (in this case, enter a date).

Image 1. Selection screen for standard As-Built Report.

 

When you execute the report, the as-built information appears in a table with multiple levels broken down in a tree visualization. By default, you see only traceable materials (serialized, batch-managed, serialized with batch, and quantity-based materials). You can see all types of materials, including backflushed and bulk materials, by choosing the “All” option.

Image 2. Main screen with output list for standard As-Built Report.

 

You can mainly use the As-Built Report to:

  • Display a multilevel as-built report based on production order or material and serial number.
  • Display all assemblies or assemblies built until a selected date.
  • See which planned and/or unplanned components have been consumed and, if alternatives are available, see if the primary or an alternative component was consumed.
  • Adjust the table layout to include more information for components / subassemblies.
  • Drill down into subassemblies of a parent assembly.

The scenario covered by the standard As-Built Report is the shop floor order scenario. Therefore, with the standard report you can only drill down into subassemblies produced with shop floor orders (the so-called shop floor items).

You can also use the As-Built Report to:

  • Display a where-used list for batch materials to check whether a batch-managed material has been used in other assemblies.
  • Display the phantom hierarchy for components that belong to a phantom assembly.
  • Make corrections to the as-built information of serialized, batch-managed, or manually posted materials, and if necessary, perform the correction without posting a goods movement.
  • Add both planned and unplanned materials to the as-built information.
  • Export the generated as-built report to Microsoft Excel.

You can display further details on the As-Built Report by doing the following:

  • Click a serial number to display the serial master record (transaction IQ03).
  • Click a batch number to display the batch master record (transaction MSC3N).
  • Click an order number in the “Order” column or in the “Where-Assembled” column to display the order details (transaction CO03).
  • Select a serialized material and choose “Product Genealogy” app to branch to the app for analyzing the production process for this specific product.
  • Select a serialized material that has a production order and choose “Order Genealogy” app to branch to the app for analyzing the production process at production order level.

 

Custom As-Built Report

Initially, several technical options were considered by the customer for implementing the As-Built custom solution:

  1. Extension of current standard PEO As-Built Report in SAP S/4HANA.
  2. Create a new interactive hierarchical ALV Report in SAP S/4HANA.
  3. Create a new Fiori App in SAP S/4HANA.
  4. Create a new Fiori App in SAP BTP.

Option 4 was discarded by customer because a UX in SAP BTP would add a lot of complexity for them. The problem with option 3 was that there was not any Fiori template available for this type of floorplan, multilevel top-down view with tree visualization. Option 1 was discarded because there were not many plans on roadmap to implement improvements (at the time of the decision), as well as option 2 was a better fit considering the great number of custom requirements.

Therefore, the final adopted approach for implementing the As-Built custom solution was to create a new classic SAPGUI app with interactive hierarchical tree visualization, as a copy of the standard PEO As-Built Report, and then add changes to cover the specific customer scenarios and features.

 

The selection screen of the new custom As-Built Report was the same as standard As-Built Report, except for new checkbox indicators available on “Selection Options” section, which allow to broaden the data scope or switching between different display modes.

Image 3. Selection screen for custom As-Built Report.

 

The main data selection can be also done either by material and serial number or by production order number, as in the standard report.

Image 4. Selection by order or material/serial number for custom As-Built Report.

 

Based on an order number or serial number and material, you can check what has been built up to the current date by default, or a selected date in case selection of radio button “Assemblies Built Until”:

Image 5. Selection until desired date for custom As-Built Report.

 

The new checkbox indicators available on “Selection Options” section allow the following:

  • Group Lines by Material: when there are several lines of same material belonging to the same reservation item, it groups the lines with a parent node.
  • Display Disassembled Records: allows to display the components disassembled on the rework modification scenarios. By default, only the reassembled components are displayed.
  • Display Non-Traceable Records: allows to display the non-traceable records, that is, the backflushed components and the bulk components.
  • Display As-Planned Records: allows to display the as-planned components, besides the real assembled components.

 

The output list format of the main screen consists of a top-down tree visualization with multiple levels, which is achieved by using an interactive hierarchical ALV report.

Image 6. Main screen with output list for custom As-Built Report.

 

The report is multilevel, which means that you can drill down into subassemblies of a parent assembly, and view key data for each material line acting as a child component and acting as a parent assembly material (if so). The complete details of the output list are explained on the following points.

 

Header Info

The header info of the standard As-Built Report displays the values entered by the user on the selection screen, by using one line for every value. As the standard header width is too big, with the aim of displaying as many lines as possible on the as-built info section, the header info of the new custom As-Built Report was condensed into just one line.

Image 7. Main screen with header info for custom As-Built Report.

 

Output List

To be able to cover all customer scenarios and provide required info of type of process and origin of the serial number/batch, the proposed solution was to follow the same approach as in standard As-Built Report of SAP S/4HANA release 2023, with one set of columns for info as a component (child component) and other set of columns for info as an assembly (parent material).

As these objects can contain shop floor orders, production orders and purchase order items, we stored the corresponding order numbers, purchase order numbers and item numbers. But, at the same time, we used two new “identification” fields for providing all the info condensed, to streamline the visualization for the users:

  • “Assembly Object”, for the assembly object à info as a parent material.
  • “Assembled Object”, for the where-assembled object à info as a child component.

Image 8. Info as a child component and as a parent material for custom As-Built Report.

 

Therefore, instead of having only one field containing the shop floor order as on the standard As-Built Report, we used two new object fields, one column “Assembly” for info as a component (child) and other column “Where-Assembled” for info as an assembly (parent).

  • “Assembly”, for the assembly object à info as a parent material.
  • “Where-Assembled”, for the where-assembled object à info as a child component.

The columns containing the order numbers, the purchase order numbers, and the purchase item numbers, both for the assembly object and for the where-assembled object, were hidden by default. However, these fields were internally used for the object navigation, depending on the type of assembly.

Image 9. Columns Assembly and Assembled for custom As-Built Report.

 

For first sight identification of the type of assembly, besides the standard field containing the traceability type (“Assembly Type”), we also included an icon field “Process” containing the type of process for the material acting as a parent assembly material. The main displayed icons identifying the type of process were the following:

Shop Floor Item (Manufacturing)

Serial Number Change (Manufacturing)

Subcontracting (Purchase)

Elements not in S4H (Reference BOM, Subcomponents)

 

When the user hovers over an icon, the corresponding description text (tooltip) is displayed. Moreover, this text was also available on text fields on the layout (by default hidden to optimize view).

Image 10. Column Type of Process (icon) and Process Text for custom As-Built Report.

 

Besides the main assembly processes, there were some special processes for which a repair or a rework activity (or more than one) were required for a component. To keep the information of this repair process, we used a new field “Repair Object”, which could contain a refurbishment subcontracting order (purchase order item) or a rework modification order (production order).

For first sight identification of the type of repair, we also had an icon field “Repair” containing the repair type. The main displayed icons identifying the type of repair were the following:

Rework-Disassembly (Manufacturing)

Rework-Reassembly (Manufacturing)

Refurbishment (Purchase)

 

Image 11. Column Type of Repair (icon) and Repair Text for custom As-Built Report.

 

With all these fields, the new report tried to cover all scenarios and all possible combinations between them, to have both information for the material as a parent material and acting as a child component.

The possible values for the column “Assembly Type” were the following (same as standard report):

  • Serialized with Batch
  • Serialized
  • Batch
  • Quantity-Based (instead of text “Manual” on standard As-Built Report 2021)
  • Backflushed
  • Bulk-Material

It is worth to mention that only “Serialized with Batch” and “Serialized” materials can act as parent assembly materials on the As-Built Report.

The “Effectivity” and “WBS Element” information was only displayed for parent nodes on the standard As-Built Report. This was changed on custom As-Built Report to show this info also for child nodes, since it made sense for grouping scenarios.

Customer has a special requirement to easily identify the migrated components on the new custom As-Built Report. Furthermore, for those migrated components with a model and unit already provided on the migration table, this info should also be displayed on column “Effectivity”.

Solution adopted was to color the column “Effectivity” in orange for those components coming from the migration and displaying model-unit info. For those components coming from the migration but without model and unit informed, text “Migrated without effectivity” was displayed instead.

Image 12. Identification of migrated components for custom As-Built Report.

 

The new column “Configuration” was added to reflect for every line component if it is a configuration element or not. The information comes from one material characteristic.

Image 13. New column Configuration for custom As-Built Report.

 

The new column “Level” was missing on the standard As-Built Report and the purpose was the same as on multi-level BOM explosion apps, to help users showing the BOM level and allow users to filter by level. The main parent assembly node starts with level 0, then its children level 1, next children level 2, and so on.

Image 14. New column Level for custom As-Built Report.

 

The new column “Special Stock” is also missing on the standard As-Built Report. As on different business units of the customer there were ETO, MTO and MTS scenarios, together with special subcontracting processes, this field can be very useful to clarify the stock type of every line.

Image 15. New column Special Stock for custom As-Built Report.

 

Navigation to the Objects

There were two types of navigations available on the new custom As-Built Report:

  • Navigation though a hotspot link (highlighted in blue), with left click of the mouse. When you hover over the cell, the field is underlined.
  • Navigation through a double click of the mouse.

For new fields “Assembly Object”, “Assembled Object” and “Repair Object”, navigation through a hotspot link was made available. This navigation was dynamic, which means that transaction called depends on the object type:

  • Transaction CO03 (Display Production Order) is called when clicking on object containing a production order.
  • Transaction ME23N (Display Purchase Order) is called when clicking on object containing a purchase order item.

For column “Serial Number”, navigation through a hotspot link was already available and transaction IQ03 (Display Material Serial Number) is called.

For column “Batch Number”, navigation through a hotspot link was already available and transaction MSC3N (Display Batch Number) is called.

For column “Material Description”, navigation through a double click was made available and transaction MM03 (Display Material Master) is called.

 

Hierarchical Tree Visualization

The hierarchical tree visualization is displayed on the left side of the ALV visualization. The “Material Number” column is displayed together with an icon and a checkbox which enables to select/deselect lines. The icons displayed on the hierarchical tree nodes were the same ones of the standard As-Built Report:

Assembly

Subassembly

Grouped Material

Planned Material

Unplanned Material

A new icon was used for the pending subcomponents on use case scenario “Elements not in SAP S/4HANA”.

Subcomponent Material (pending info)

 

When the initial selection on selection screen is done by material and serial number, only one assembly is displayed on the tree. However, when the selection is done by order, more than one assembly can be displayed in case orders containing more than one serial number.

Image 16. Hierarchical tree visualization for custom As-Built Report.

 

Toolbar Buttons and Actions

The available buttons on standard As-Built Report toolbar are the following:

Image 17. Toolbar buttons for standard As-Built Report.

 

More buttons were added to the new custom As-Built Report, which are the following:

Image 18. Toolbar buttons for custom As-Built Report.

 

On the hierarchical tree visualization, there is a selection checkbox available for any record on the “Material Number” column, between the icon and the material number, which enables to select and deselect the line.

Some buttons apply for all the report and can be used without any line selection, like “Refresh” or “Export”. However, some other buttons require a selected line to be enabled for execution. When the line is selected, the corresponding button actions are enabled, which means that you can execute them for the corresponding selected line.

Image 19. Enabled buttons when line selected for custom As-Built Report.

 

As there are no actions available to be performed for more than one line, selection of more than one line is not allowed, and corresponding error message “Please select one item only” would be displayed.

The functionality of the toolbar buttons of the new custom As-Built Report is the following:

  • The “Refresh” button allows to refresh all the report without exit to the selection screen.
  • The “Optimize Columns” button allows to automatically optimize the width for all the columns/fields displayed.
  • The “Expand Subtree” and “Collapse Subtree” buttons allow to open and close the main parent node, and moreover the component nodes which are in turn parent materials as well. The corresponding BOM level is displayed on column “Level”.
  • The “Export” button allows to export data to Excel format. The internal IDs of the serialized materials allow to identify the hierarchical relationship between assembled components. A custom layout was developed ad-hoc to be used for the As-Maintained Report, which was managed on external maintenance system and used the spreadsheet exported As-Built info as a basis.
  • The “Where-Used List” button is only available for lines with batches and allow to navigate to the where-used list of the corresponding batch.
  • The “Genealogy” button is only available for lines with serial numbers assembled in a shop floor order (shop floor items). This button allows to navigate to the product genealogy app (F2892) or order genealogy app (F5865).
  • The “Correction” button allows to make corrections to the as-built information of serialized, batch-managed, or manually posted materials. If you identify that the wrong serial number, batch number, or quantity was recorded for an assembled material, you can correct this data to resolve any inconsistencies. All corrections were performed without posting a goods movement as per customer requirement.

Image 20. Correction button options when line selected for custom As-Built Report.

 

Group Lines by Material

By default, when there are several lines of the same component belonging to the same reservation item, the standard As-Built Report groups the lines with a parent node displayed with the icon  “Grouped Material”. The total planned and consumed quantity is displayed on parent node, while specific consumed quantity is displayed for every line without any planned quantity.

Image 21. Example of nodes with grouped material for standard As-Built Report.

 

Customer requested the possibility of displaying those lines without grouping. To allow both scenarios, a new checkbox indicator “Group Lines by Material” was made available on the selection screen, to enable grouping or not according to user preferences.

Image 22. Selection option Group Lines by Material for custom As-Built Report.

Image 23. Visualization with material grouping for custom As-Built Report.

Image 24. Visualization without material grouping for custom As-Built Report.

 

The visualization without material grouping was selected as default option by customer.

 

Display Disassembled Records

The new checkbox “Display Disassembled Records” on Selection Options allows to display the components disassembled on the rework modification scenarios. By default, only the reassembled components are displayed.

Image 25. Visualization without disassembled records for custom As-Built Report.

 

Just in case you want to also display the disassembled components together with the corresponding reassembled components, you can mark the checkbox “Display Disassembled Records”:

Image 26. Selection option Display Disassembled Records for custom As-Built Report.

 

For a convenient display and differentiation of the disassembled component lines, they are displayed blurred in red color, and the “Consumed Qty” field is also marked with red color to highlight that quantity it’s zero (0 PC).

Image 27. Visualization with disassembled records for custom As-Built Report.

 

Display Non-Traceable Records

The non-traceable materials are the bulk components and the backflushed components. A new checkbox “Display Non-Traceable Records” was included on the selection screen to enable/disable their visualization. The idea is that you can select when to display or not the non-traceable records, thus increasing the report performance when not required.

Therefore, in case you want to also display the non-traceable components together with the traceable components, you can mark the checkbox “Display Non-Traceable Records”:

Image 28. Selection option Display Non-Traceable Records for custom As-Built Report.

 

When the checkbox is marked, two new buttons are available on the report (as happens in the standard As-Built Report) to enable the switching between:

  • Button “All”: display all the components.
  • Button “Traceable”: display only the traceable components.

The report displays on the corresponding buttons the total number of lines and the lines only for traceable items, so the difference is the non-traceable. The assembly types for non-traceable components are “Backflushed” and “Bulk material”.

Image 29. Visualization with non-traceable records for custom As-Built Report.

 

Custom As-Built Report with Shop Floor Orders

The shop floor order scenario is the one covered by the standard As-Built Report. With the standard report you can only drill down into subassemblies produced with shop floor orders (the so-called shop floor items). To be able to differentiate this scenario from others, new identification fields were used, as explained on previous “Output List” point.

For the material acting a parent assembly material, the “Assembly” object is the shop floor order when the material has been produced. The icon field containing the type of assembly process for manufacturing is  “Shop Floor Item”. For the material acting as child component, the “Where-Assembled” object is the shop floor order where consumed.

Image 30. Example of shop floor order scenario for custom As-Built Report.

 

The “Consumed Qty” will be quantity one ‘1’ in case the production status of the corresponding serial number is “Completed” or “Scrapped”. For the rest of status (for instance, “In Process”, “In Queue”, and “Not in Production”), quantity shown will be zero ‘0’ highlighted in yellow color, to warn about the fact that the serial number postings have not yet been completed. This behavior could be changed as per other customer expected behavior (for instance, display only completed or scrapped serial numbers).

Image 31. Shop floor order scenario with empty consumed qty for custom As-Built Report.

 

If the component material should be a parent assembly material because the material has been produced with a shop floor order, but there are not any components consumed yet, the line will be displayed with the corresponding icon and texts containing the type of assembly process, but the “Expand”  icon will not be available, as there are no children components yet.

Image 32. Parent assembly material without components for custom As-Built Report.

 

Helpful Links

As-Built Report (SAP Help Portal)

As-Built Report (SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library)

Incident component for clarifications: PP-PEO-SFE

 

 

[Disclaimer: Written by Juan Ventura (LinkedIn). This blog post is written by the author based on his personal experience while working on customers with SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud. Opinions and views expressed herein are solely my own. Before implementing any solution, you are advised to always do the analysis and adopt a solution for specific business case only if it suits customer requirements and testing is successful.]

entry.title: Re: Unable to Set Payment Terms and Payment Method for Ve...
entry.content:
Dear Dawid,

Thank you for your reply.
We have already checked SSCUI 102923 – Define Tolerances for Customers and Suppliers and have not made any changes from the default configuration.
For Default Tolerance Cust/DefVend, we believe that the "Payment Term from Invoice" indicator is enabled. In addition, the tolerance group assigned to both the customer and supplier involved in the clearing process is left blank, which means the default tolerance group (Default Tolerance Cust/Vend) is being used.
Under these conditions, when we create a supplier residual item, the payment terms from the original supplier liability are not inherited by the generated residual item.
We also tested maintaining a fixed value in the "Fixed Payment Term" field and then creating a supplier residual item again. However, the payment terms were still not populated on the residual item.
Could you please advise whether there is any additional configuration, prerequisite, or setting that we may have overlooked?

entry.title: Re: FBL1N mass block
entry.content:
Read those KBA:

  • 3653266 - Mass Change for field payment block does not work in FBL1N. 
    • 1700256 - MSITEM006 occurs when mass change payment block in FBL1N 
    • 3681453 - Unable to remove payment block manually to perform clearing 
    • 3622285 - Baseline date not changing in the accounting document in FB02 

The mass change functionality in transaction FBL1N has limitations, particularly when modifying baseline dates and certain payment block adjustments. To ensure proper processing, it is recommended to first update the baseline date at the invoice level before affecting related items. Additionally, if mass changes are unsuccessful, consider making payment block modifications via transaction FB02.

entry.title: SAP IAG PAM – How Does Fiori Launchpad (/UI2/FLP) Work Within a Privileged Access in S/4HANA PCE?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are implementing SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG) Privileged Access Management (PAM) with SAP S/4HANA PCE 2023

I would like to understand the Fiori use case for Privileged Access IDs.

  • A user launches a PAM session (SIAG_PAM_LAUNCH_PAD) using a Privileged Access ID and then wants to perform activities using the SAP Fiori Launchpad (/UI2/FLP) instead of SAP GUI.

My questions are:

  • If the user accesses /UI2/FLP during an active PAM session, will the Fiori Launchpad run with the Privileged Access ID's authorizations or continue to use the physical user's SSO identity?
  • Is there a SAP-supported approach for using Fiori applications under a PAM ID?
  • If the Privileged Access ID has Fiori business roles assigned, can those roles be utilized through the PAM session?
  • Are there any additional configurations or best practices required to enable privileged activities in Fiori using SAP IAG PAM?

We are trying to understand the recommended approach for users who need to perform privileged activities through Fiori applications rather than SAP GUI transactions. Thank you in advance for your guidance.

Best Regards,

Vijay

entry.title: Re: Deprecation of SOAP APIs for Asset Master Data and Co...
entry.content:
Good day @t-sakai 

Thank you for your question.

There is no exact decommissioning date that can be confirmed. A depreciation announcement means the API or communication scenario is planned for removal in the future, but it is not the same as a confirmed deletion date.

The 12-month rule for deprecated apps does not automatically define the exact removal date for APIs.

See App Lifecycle, Replacement, and Deprecation and APIs and Events Deprecation Policy for more details.

So, the safe conclusion is:

  1. The SOAP APIs and SAP_COM_0563 are deprecated, not necessarily already decommissioned.
  2. The earliest possible decommissioning will be done according to API depreciation policy.
  3. The exact decommissioning date will be published in SAP API documentation, SAP Business Accelerator Hub, or a specific SAP Note/KBA.

I recommend not to further invest in the SOAP based asset master data integration. Fixed Asset-Master Data (API_FIXEDASSET_G4BA) with protocol OData V4 is provided, and you should use communication scenario SAP_COM_0A93 for new projects because it provides more precise, target specific authorisation control.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: I would like a feature that performs a check when sav...
entry.content:
Good day @ISoma 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, you can normally handle this through custom logic, not through a standard item category field control setting.

In your case, the logic would be:

If Item Category= SWE2 or SVP2 and ParentServiceOderItem is initial then raise an error message and stop the save.

Note that Manage Service Orders app is used to display, create, and edit service orders. ParentServiceOrderItem is also exposed as the parent service order item field in the Service Order API item structure. For item hierarchies, the parent item must be specified when creating an item hierarchy through the Service Order API.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/adbae5bcd5994f159bf2847a11397b61/79d61a4756ba470fbba3f6cb32becaa8.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/adbae5bcd5994f159bf2847a11397b61/1209e1ca48c04105a08ab2314887f4ad.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6d57d4ab71e5408581e2018320a0ce49/17b5f83deff94d2e9572f0073bf0ee37.html?locale=en-US

What is recommended is for you to use Custom Logic and create the implementation for the service transaction check extension point.

  1. Open Custom Logic, create an implementation for the service transaction check BAdI.                                                          

In the implementation, check the service order item data for:

  • Item category = SWE2 or SVP2
  • Parent service order item = blank

 

  1. Return an error message when the condition is met and publish the implementation.

 

  1. Then in Manage Service Orders, change a service order with an item category SWE2 or SVP2 and leave the parent item blank. Make sure you save.

 

The expected result should be that the service order is not saved and the custom error message is displayed.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/adbae5bcd5994f159bf2847a11397b61/ca2aca50a01e44c79bd1f1a15f465700.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: 999 – The Number of the BSEG
entry.content:
Hello Jitka,

yes, absolutely, for the notorious India GST case (i.e. invoices in an Indian company code containing Indian GST condition types), activating the old BAdi FI_BILL_ISSUE_SPLIT is still the only available workaround we have. As you correctly stated, Indian tax authorities require a strict line item wise tax calculation for these GSTs (and SAP requires a clear line item linkage between tax reporting table BSET and BSEG ;-)), so this is indeed a special situation where we cannot summarize the resulting BSEG items, but have to go for the invoice split workaround. By the way, in the Public Cloud edition we also use this invoice split workaround for the Indian GST case.

Good to hear you could solve your cash discount issue by summarization!

Best regards, 

Stefan

entry.title: Re: Connecting Two SAP CAP Projects Without Duplicating Your Data
entry.content:
@junwu Yes UPS is needed,

HDI generates the schema name at deploy time — you don't get to choose it. The UPS is just how you pass that dynamic name over to Project B's deployer. Without it, there's no way to wire the two containers together cleanly.

You can hardcode the schema name, but it'll break the moment you redeploy or switch environments.

entry.title: Re: Connecting Two SAP CAP Projects Without Duplicating Your Data
entry.content: @vbalko-claimate HDI synonyms are HANA-specific, so the whole UPS + synonym setup doesn't apply on Postgres.

entry.title: Start a Career in SAP Consulting: A Structured Path on Coursera
entry.content:
The SAP Technology Consultant role sits at the heart of most implementations, translating business requirements into solutions and guiding projects from design through delivery. It’s also one of the most in-demand roles in the SAP ecosystem. 

For those looking to move into this space, the SAP Technology Consultant Professional Certificate on Coursera offers a clear and accessible starting point, no prior SAP experience needed. 

What the Coursera certificate covers 

The curriculum goes beyond product knowledge. Across seven courses, learners cover consulting skills, project management and tools like SAP S/4HANA A full hands-on implementation project puts it all into practice, producing real deliverables learners can mention in interviews. The final course focuses on career development and preparation for SAP Certification. 

Each course builds on the previous one. By the capstone, learners have worked through the full SAP implementation lifecycle twice: once with guidance, once independently. The certificate also carries ACE (American Council on Education) credit recommendations, meaning learners may be able to apply their learning toward academic credit at participating U.S. and Latin American universities. 

Who this is for 

The Coursera certificate is a strong starting point for anyone looking to enter SAP consulting. It's also useful for people already in the ecosystem who work alongside consultants and want a clearer picture of the full project lifecycle. 

From Coursera certificate to SAP Certification 

Completing the Coursera certificate is just the beginning. It builds foundational knowledge, with learners then deepening skills through SAP Learning site via self-paced courses and expert-led journeys. 

 Start learning on Coursera now: SAP Technology Consultant Professional Certificate

entry.title: SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer | Preparation Guide & How to Ace It (C_AIG_2604)
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer credential (exam code C_AIG_2604) is one of SAP's newest and most hands-on certifications. Unlike traditional multiple-choice SAP exams, this one is a 3-hour System-Based Assessment (SBA) where you actually build prompt templates, orchestrate LLMs, and produce structured JSON outputs inside the SAP AI Launchpad — the exact tools a real Generative AI Developer uses on the job.

I recently took the exam and cleared it with a 100% score (cut score: 76%). In this blog, I'm walking you through everything — the learning journey, what the exam really looks like, task-by-task strategy, the pitfalls I discovered live, and the tips that helped me ace it.

Learning Journey link: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/solving-business-problems-using-sap-s-generative-ai-hub

About the Certification

Attribute

Detail

Exam Code

C_AIG_2604

Title

SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer

Format

System-Based Assessment (hands-on, open-book)

Duration

3 hours (countdown starts as soon as you click Access)

Delivery

Remote, via SAP Learning platform

Attempts

4 attempts within 12 months

Cut Score

76%

Primary System

SAP AI Launchpad – Generative AI Hub

Reference Tool

SAP Joule for Consultants (available during the exam)

 

What makes this exam different is that there are no MCQs. You are dropped into a live SAP AI Launchpad tenant and asked to design, refine, evaluate and productionize prompt templates — all evaluated by the outputs and templates you leave behind in the orchestration scenario.

The Learning Journey – What to Study

The official SAP Learning Journey — "Solving Business Problems Using SAP's Generative AI Hub" — is the single most important preparation resource. Everything in the exam is a variation of what you practice there. Focus especially on:

  • Generative AI Hub fundamentals – Model Library, Prompt Editor, Prompt Management, Orchestration, Optimizations.
  • Prompt Templates vs Prompts – understand the difference. The exam explicitly asks for prompt templates saved in the orchestration scenario, not simple prompt editor sessions.
  • System / User / Assistant roles – how to structure them, when to use the assistant role for tracking output evolution.
  • Variables & placeholders – syntax like {{user_email_placeholder}} and {{?input_var1}}.
  • Structured JSON outputs – schema-driven prompting (Feedback_ID, Main_Category, Customer_Sentiment, Issue_Summary, etc.).
  • Model comparison – be comfortable comparing Gemini, GPT-4o / GPT-5, GPT nano, Claude etc. and justifying which one produces the best structured output.
  • Orchestration workflows – templating, versioning, and saving in the orchestration scenario.
  • Prompt optimization – how the Optimize Prompt feature works and when to use it.

 

Practical tip: don't just read the exercises — actually complete every single hands-on exercise in your trial tenant. The muscle memory of navigating Prompt Management, saving revisions and testing outputs is what saves you time in the exam.

Exam-Day Setup – What You Actually See

Once your slot is confirmed (mine was July 3rd, 2026, 15:00 Europe/London), you get a confirmation email from SAP Notifications with your Enrollment ID. On the day, log in a few minutes early — you get a 15-minute grace period, after which you lose the slot.

The Certification Exam page has three key steps:

  1. Step 1: Access – opens the practice/exam system in a new tab. The 3-hour countdown starts the moment you click Access.
  2. Step 2: Download the Certification Task_EN_EN PDF – this is your task brief. Read it before touching anything.
  3. Step 3: Confirm Completion – only click this when you are 100% done. There is no undo.

 

  • Joule for Consultants is now available in the systems menu alongside the AI Launchpad. Use it as your open-book assistant during the exam — it can help clarify SAP concepts, but it will not do the tasks for you.

Critical Rules You Must Not Miss

These are the small details that cost people points. Read them twice:

  • Do NOT click "Confirm Completion" until every task is saved and verified. That button ends the exam.
  • Group Number formatting is strict. The instructions say: replace redundant hashtags with leading zeros. If your group number is 2, you write 02 (not #2, not 2). Any capitalisation, spaces or symbol mismatch costs marks.
  • Template naming is exact. The naming convention is feedback-analyzer-<GroupName>. E.g., my group was CERT4495, so my template was feedback-analyzer-CERT4495. A typo here means the reviewer can't find your work.
  • Save as a Prompt Template, not a Prompt. Prompt Editor sessions do not count. You must save each version to Prompt Management under the orchestration scenario.
  • Version discipline. Task 1 = v1.0.0, Task 2 = v2.0.0, Task 3 = v3.0.0, Task 4 = v4.0.0 (with minor patches like 1.1.0, 1.2.0 as you iterate). Reviewers evaluate the final version of each.
  • JSON output must be pure JSON. No markdown fences, no "Here is the JSON:" preamble. Only the JSON object.
  • Assistant role for traceability. Save your best AI response into the Assistant role of the template — this is how the reviewer traces your solution's evolution.
  • Note down your Group Number & Group ID immediately and paste them into Joule/Notes so you don't have to hunt for them later.

The Business Scenario

Everything in the exam revolves around a scenario similar to the practice exercise. The four tasks progressively deepen the same use case — starting from a basic prompt template, going all the way to a multi-issue prioritization workflow.

Task-by-Task Summary

Task

What You Build

Est. Time

1

Initial prompt template for core feedback insights 

25 min

2

Refine accuracy & add semantic depth for problem resolution

35 min

3

Evaluate and select the optimal LLM for production

35 min

4

Advanced multi-issue extraction & prioritization template

60 min

5

Submit the assessment (Confirm Completion)

5 min

 

Submitting the Assessment

Once every template (v1.0.0 → v4.0.0, plus any minor patches) is saved in Prompt Management under the orchestration scenario, and the Assistant role of each template has your best validated JSON pasted in:

  1. Go back to the Certification Exam tab in SAP Learning.
  2. Click Confirm Completion.
  3. You'll see the "System Based Assessment submitted and pending review" screen.
  4. Review usually completes in about 15 minutes. In my case the result was on my certification page shortly after — 100% score.

Pro Tips to Ace It

  • Practice the exercises end-to-end in a trial Generative AI Hub tenant. Nothing beats muscle memory in a hands-on exam.
  • Keep the sample messages open in a side tab. You will use them constantly for testing.
  • Use an external JSON validator (e.g., jsonformatter.curiousconcept.com) to be sure your output is RFC 8259 compliant before pasting into the Assistant role. Link - https://jsonformatter.curiousconcept.com/#
  • Version early, version often. Save minor patches (1.1.0, 1.2.0…) as you iterate — reviewers see your progression and it protects you if a later save is corrupted.
  • Read the instructions verbatim. The PDF is intentionally precise. "Prompt template" ≠ "prompt". "Orchestration scenario" is mandatory. Group number formatting is strict.
  • Keep Joule for Consultants open. Use it for concept clarifications, not to write your prompts. Reviewers grade YOUR templates.
  • Do not click Confirm Completion until you're truly done. There is no undo, and the countdown is generous enough for careful review.
  • Manage your time. Give Task 1 up to an hour — the design decisions there carry through to every other task. Tasks 2 & 3 are quick if Task 1 is solid. Reserve 45–60 minutes for Task 4.

Final Thoughts

The C_AIG_2604 exam is genuinely enjoyable if you love prompt engineering. It rewards clarity of thinking, disciplined JSON schema design, and careful iteration — the same skills you use every day building GenAI solutions on SAP BTP. Once you finish Task 1 correctly, the rest of the exam becomes an exercise in progressively enriching a well-designed template.

If you've completed the SAP Learning Journey exercises, treat the exam as "Exercise 5" — same tools, same patterns, higher stakes. Trust the process, mind the details, and you'll walk out of the 3 hours with a certification that genuinely reflects real-world Generative AI Developer skills.

 

Good luck — and see you on the certified list! 🎉
